entries.title: GLP-1 receptor agonists in stroke prevention: a narrative review on emerging therapeutic frontiers.
entries.displayTitle: GLP-1 agonists may cut ischemic stroke risk by up to 39%
entries.summary: Narrative review of cardiovascular outcome trials and mechanistic studies examining GLP-1 receptor agonists for stroke prevention in type 2 diabetes. Long-acting agents show 15–39% relative risk reductions for nonfatal and ischemic stroke through anti-inflammatory, neuroprotective, and endothelial mechanisms.
keyFindings: Long-acting GLP-1RAs reduce nonfatal and ischemic stroke risk by 15–39% across major trials
Benefits appear independent of glycemic control, strongest in preserved renal function and shorter diabetes duration
Short-acting exendin-based GLP-1RAs show limited cerebrovascular benefit
Mechanisms include anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, neuroprotective, and endothelial-stabilizing effects
entries.limitations: Inconsistencies across trials and limited data in non-diabetic or high-risk populations; narrative review methodology without systematic search or meta-analysis.
entries.context: This review synthesizes emerging evidence that GLP-1RAs provide cerebrovascular protection beyond glucose control in type 2 diabetes. The multi-mechanism hypothesis extends prior observations of cardiovascular risk reduction to specific stroke endpoints.

entries.title: Unexplained hypercapnia with normal pulmonary evaluation in a patient receiving semaglutide: a diagnostic challenge.
entries.displayTitle: Case report: rare central hypercapnia with semaglutide
entries.summary: A 40-year-old patient without diabetes developed reversible central respiratory depression with hypercapnia four weeks after escalating semaglutide to 1 mg weekly. Symptoms and blood gas abnormalities resolved within 3 weeks of discontinuation.
keyFindings: Patient developed respiratory acidosis (pH 7.33, PaCO₂ 56 mmHg) 4 weeks after semaglutide dose escalation to 1 mg weekly
Capnography showed sustained nocturnal hypercapnia without apnea (AHI <5), suggesting impaired central ventilatory drive
Symptoms and arterial blood gas normalized within 3 weeks of semaglutide discontinuation
All conventional causes of hypercapnia were excluded through comprehensive evaluation
entries.limitations: Single case report; causality cannot be definitively established without rechallenge or larger case series.
entries.context: This appears to be a previously unreported adverse event. GLP-1 receptor agonists like semaglutide are widely used for obesity management, but respiratory complications are rarely documented. This case suggests a potential central respiratory depression mechanism.

entries.title: GLP-1 agonists and changes in body mass and composition in adults with overweight or obesity with or without type 2 diabetes mellitus: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
entries.displayTitle: GLP-1 agonists reduce fat mass while preserving lean tissue in obesity
entries.summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis of 36 studies found that GLP-1 receptor agonists and dual GLP-1/GIP agonists produce clinically meaningful weight loss (4–9% over 3–12 months) primarily through selective fat mass reduction, with relative preservation of lean body mass in people with overweight or obesity.
keyFindings: At 3 months, mean body weight decreased by approximately 9%, with marked reductions in fat mass and visceral adipose tissue.
At 6 months, weight reduction averaged 5%, with semaglutide, liraglutide, and exenatide showing comparable effects.
At 12 months, weight loss persisted at around 4%, with fat mass decline predominating over lean mass reductions.
Lean body mass remained largely preserved across all time points, supporting 'quality' weight loss.
entries.limitations: The meta-analysis included studies with varying durations, populations (with/without diabetes), and GLP-1 agents, which may contribute to heterogeneity in effect sizes and limit direct comparisons.
entries.context: This review extends prior knowledge by systematically quantifying body composition changes with GLP-1 agonists, confirming that weight loss is primarily fat-driven rather than lean tissue loss. The preservation of lean mass is clinically important for metabolic health and distinguishes these agents from non-selective weight loss approaches.

entries.title: Cardiovascular outcomes of glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists: A systematic review.
entries.displayTitle: GLP-1 agonists reduce major cardiac events across diabetes and obesity
entries.journal: American journal of health-system pharmacy : AJHP : official journal of the American Society of Health-System Pharmacists
entries.summary: Systematic review of 12 cardiovascular outcome trials found GLP-1 receptor agonists consistently reduced major adverse cardiovascular events in type 2 diabetes patients. Benefits extended to patients with overweight/obesity and cardiovascular disease without diabetes, and those with obesity and heart failure with preserved ejection fraction.
keyFindings: GLP-1RAs consistently reduced MACE in patients with type 2 diabetes across 10 trials
Semaglutide lowered MACE risk in overweight/obesity with cardiovascular disease but no diabetes
Tirzepatide reduced heart failure events and cardiovascular death in obesity with HFpEF
Only liraglutide individually demonstrated reductions in MACE, cardiovascular death, and all-cause death
entries.limitations: Review appears to include trials with varying follow-up durations (1.2 to 5.4 years) and heterogeneous patient populations, which may limit direct comparisons across studies.
entries.context: This review confirms and extends the established cardiovascular benefits of GLP-1 receptor agonists beyond type 2 diabetes. The findings are consistent with prior individual trials but notably synthesize evidence across diverse populations, including those without diabetes, expanding the potential therapeutic scope of these peptides.

entries.title: Real-World Comparison of Short-Term Adverse Events, Treatment Persistence, and Efficacy of Semaglutide and Tirzepatide: A Nationwide Multicenter Study.
entries.displayTitle: Tirzepatide produces 14.4% weight loss at 6 months vs 12.6% with semaglutide
entries.summary: This nationwide observational study of 2,549 patients with obesity compared semaglutide and tirzepatide over 6 months. Adverse event rates were similar (~51%), but tirzepatide showed greater weight loss at all timepoints despite more musculoskeletal and allergic reactions.
keyFindings: Adverse events occurred in ~51% of both groups, with gastrointestinal events most common in both
Tirzepatide produced 14.4% median body weight loss at 6 months versus 12.6% with semaglutide
Musculoskeletal and allergic reactions were more frequent with tirzepatide
Pancreatic-event discontinuation was more common with semaglutide (p=0.006)
entries.limitations: Observational design without randomization; short 6-month follow-up does not capture long-term safety or weight maintenance outcomes.
entries.context: This real-world comparison extends prior trial findings showing tirzepatide's superior weight loss efficacy. The similar overall adverse-event rates and persistence align with known safety profiles, though musculoskeletal and allergic findings with tirzepatide warrant further investigation.

entries.title: Efficacy and safety of semaglutide injection in Indian patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus inadequately controlled on metformin: a phase 3, randomized, active-controlled trial (SIZE-DM study).
entries.displayTitle: Generic semaglutide matches innovator brand in Indian T2DM patients
entries.summary: A 24-week randomized trial in 320 Indian adults with type 2 diabetes on metformin found that generic semaglutide (Alkem) was non-inferior to innovator semaglutide (Novo Nordisk), achieving mean HbA1c reductions of 2.20% with comparable safety profiles.
keyFindings: Both generic and innovator semaglutide reduced HbA1c by mean 2.20% at 24 weeks
86.62% of participants achieved HbA1c < 7.0% target
Body weight, fasting and post-prandial glucose reductions were similar between groups
Safety profiles comparable with predominantly mild-to-moderate adverse events; no treatment-related serious adverse events
entries.limitations: Study limited to Indian population with 24-week duration; longer-term outcomes and cardiovascular endpoints were not assessed.
entries.context: This is the first non-inferiority trial comparing generic to innovator semaglutide. The 2.20% HbA1c reduction is consistent with previous semaglutide trials in diverse populations, supporting biosimilarity of the generic formulation.

entries.title: Glucagon-like peptide-1 and atrial fibrillation incidence: Associations independent of weight loss.
entries.displayTitle: Semaglutide cuts atrial fibrillation risk by 33% independent of weight loss
entries.summary: A retrospective cohort study of 12,812 patients found GLP-1 receptor agonists reduced atrial fibrillation incidence by 33% and mortality by 66% compared to matched controls, with benefits persisting even in patients who gained weight. Among GLP-1 agents, only semaglutide showed statistically significant AF risk reduction.
keyFindings: GLP-1 use associated with 33% lower AF risk (HR 0.67) and 66% lower mortality (HR 0.34) versus controls
AF protection persisted across all weight change groups, including those who gained weight
Only semaglutide showed statistically significant AF risk reduction among GLP-1 agents tested
AF risk reduction became apparent after 24 months of continuous GLP-1 use
entries.limitations: Retrospective design from single academic center; AF detection via chart review may miss asymptomatic cases; residual confounding possible despite propensity matching.
entries.context: Prior studies have linked GLP-1 agonists to reduced AF incidence, but mechanisms were unclear. This study extends prior findings by demonstrating AF benefits independent of weight loss, suggesting direct antiarrhythmic properties beyond metabolic effects.

entries.title: Efficacy, Safety and PK of Once-Daily Oral Semaglutide 25 mg for Obesity With and Without Type 2 Diabetes in Comparison With Subcutaneous Semaglutide 2.4 mg: A Model-Informed Drug Development Approach.
entries.displayTitle: Oral semaglutide 25 mg matches subcutaneous 2.4 mg for weight loss via model-based analysis
entries.summary: Model-informed drug development analysis compared once-daily oral semaglutide 25 mg with subcutaneous semaglutide 2.4 mg for obesity with and without type 2 diabetes. Virtual trials showed oral semaglutide 25 mg achieved similar exposure levels and weight reduction as subcutaneous formulation.
keyFindings: 82.2% of participants on oral semaglutide 25 mg achieved exposure levels comparable to subcutaneous 2.4 mg weekly
Simulated weight reduction with oral 25 mg: 9.4% in obesity+T2D; 16.3% in obesity without T2D
Weight loss efficacy of oral 25 mg matched subcutaneous 2.4 mg across populations
entries.limitations: Efficacy estimates derived from virtual/simulated trials rather than prospective randomized controlled trials; real-world safety and efficacy data still needed.
entries.context: This pharmacokinetic modeling approach supported regulatory approval of oral semaglutide 25 mg without conducting full Phase 2/3 trials. It extends semaglutide's weight-management applications by demonstrating bioequivalence between oral and subcutaneous formulations at therapeutically relevant exposures.

entries.title: LEAN mass Preservation with Resistance Exercise and Protein during semaglutide and tirzepatide therapy (LEAN-PREP study): a protocol for a randomised controlled trial.
entries.displayTitle: Trial tests resistance exercise and protein to preserve muscle during semaglutide and tirzepatide
entries.summary: This protocol describes a 6-month randomized trial in 232 adults with obesity in Kuwait testing whether resistance exercise and/or protein supplementation (1.6 g/kg/day) can preserve muscle mass and function during semaglutide or tirzepatide therapy. Primary outcome is MRI-measured quadriceps cross-sectional area.
keyFindings: Trial will randomize 232 adults with obesity to control, exercise, protein, or combined intervention during GLP-1 therapy
Home-based resistance training will involve 3 sessions weekly targeting major muscle groups
Protein supplementation will target 1.6 g/kg/day via dietary adjustment and supplements
Primary outcome is MRI-measured quadriceps cross-sectional area at 6 months
entries.limitations: This is a protocol only; no results are yet available to assess intervention efficacy.
entries.context: GLP-1 receptor agonists produce substantial weight loss but also significant lean mass loss, raising sarcopenia concerns. This trial addresses a recognized gap by testing pragmatic interventions to preserve muscle during therapy, particularly relevant in Kuwait where muscle weakness prevalence is reportedly high in people with type 2 diabetes.

entries.title: Is There a Causal Link Between GLP-1 Receptor Agonists and Non-Arteritic Anterior Ischaemic Optic Neuropathy? A Critique of the Clinical Evidence.
entries.displayTitle: Review finds limited evidence linking GLP-1 RAs to rare vision loss
entries.summary: This critical review examines whether GLP-1 receptor agonists, particularly semaglutide, cause non-arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (NAION). Observational data show mixed findings, and meta-analyses of RCTs lack power to assess this rare outcome definitively.
keyFindings: Scandinavian registries report elevated NAION risk; U.S. and multinational databases show null or weak associations
Meta-analyses of RCTs suggest at most a moderate risk increase (OR 2–3) but lack power
Authors conclude causal link cannot be excluded but requires larger pooled RCT analyses with adjudicated outcomes
entries.limitations: Review acknowledges current RCT meta-analyses lack statistical power to definitively assess this rare outcome; relies heavily on heterogeneous observational data.
entries.context: This review addresses emerging pharmacovigilance signals about GLP-1 RA–associated vision loss. It appears consistent with prior safety reviews acknowledging uncertainty around rare adverse events that require large datasets to detect reliably.

entries.title: Semaglutide reduces murine blood pressure through the vascular smooth muscle GLP-1 receptor.
entries.displayTitle: Semaglutide lowers blood pressure via vascular smooth muscle GLP-1 receptors in mice
entries.summary: This mouse study demonstrates that GLP-1 receptors on vascular smooth muscle cells (VSMCs), not endothelial cells, are essential for semaglutide's blood pressure–lowering effects. VSMC GLP-1R also mediates increased glomerular filtration and natriuresis but is not required for metabolic effects.
keyFindings: VSMC GLP-1R deletion abolished semaglutide's blood pressure reduction; endothelial or immune cell GLP-1R were not required
VSMC GLP-1R mediates increased glomerular filtration rate and sodium excretion but not metabolic effects
Semaglutide directly induced vasorelaxation in preconstricted mesenteric arteries ex vivo
VSMC GLP-1R deletion prevented semaglutide-induced proteomic changes in renal artery and kidney
entries.limitations: Findings are from murine models; translation to human vascular physiology and blood pressure regulation requires clinical validation.
entries.context: Blood pressure reduction with GLP-1 agonists was previously attributed to weight loss or endothelial signaling. This study reveals a novel mechanism through direct VSMC GLP-1R activation, linking cardiovascular and renal benefits independently of metabolic effects.

entries.title: Semaglutide for obesity management: A narrative review of efficacy, safety, and future directions.
entries.displayTitle: Narrative review examines semaglutide safety, adherence, and use in special populations
entries.journal: Journal of the American Pharmacists Association : JAPhA
entries.summary: This narrative review synthesized evidence from 34 studies on semaglutide's efficacy and safety for obesity management, focusing on adverse events, treatment persistence, perioperative considerations, and use in special populations including Alzheimer's disease and post-bariatric surgery patients.
keyFindings: Clinical trials consistently demonstrated meaningful weight reduction with semaglutide across diverse populations
Evidence on acute pancreatitis remains limited; evidence on suicidal ideation is mixed with conflicting safety signals
Recent guidelines support individualized perioperative assessment for GLP-1 RA management
Long-term neurologic and post-bariatric safety outcomes remain incompletely characterized
entries.limitations: Narrative review methodology does not involve systematic evidence grading or meta-analysis; conclusions drawn from mixed-quality evidence including observational studies and case reports.
entries.context: This review synthesizes emerging real-world evidence as semaglutide use expands beyond traditional diabetes and obesity populations. It addresses growing clinical concerns about long-term safety, adherence, and appropriate use in understudied patient groups.

entries.title: Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists and Risk of Systemic and Ocular Vascular Complications in Patients with Type 2 Diabetes and Diabetic Retinopathy.
entries.displayTitle: GLP-1 agonists cut cardiovascular and retinal risks in diabetics with retinopathy
entries.summary: Retrospective cohort study of 173,216 adults with type 2 diabetes and diabetic retinopathy found GLP-1 receptor agonist use was associated with significantly reduced risks of cardiovascular events, kidney complications, and retinal vascular complications over two years.
keyFindings: 35% lower myocardial infarction risk (HR 0.65) and 22% lower ischemic stroke risk (HR 0.78) with GLP-1 RA use
60% reduction in need for renal replacement therapy (HR 0.40) and 32% lower acute kidney injury risk (HR 0.68)
22% lower progression to proliferative diabetic retinopathy (HR 0.78) and 30% lower retinal vein occlusion risk (HR 0.70)
No significant association observed for retinal artery occlusions or non-arteritic ischemic optic neuropathy
entries.limitations: Retrospective design limits causal inference; residual confounding possible despite propensity score matching; prescription patterns may not reflect actual medication adherence.
entries.context: This extends known cardiovascular and renal benefits of GLP-1 RAs to a high-risk population with pre-existing diabetic retinopathy, a group often excluded from clinical trials. The observed retinal vascular benefits are consistent with broader microvascular protective effects.

entries.title: Differential Effects of Semaglutide and Colchicine on Atrial Remodeling in Rats with Reduced Ejection Fraction after Myocardial Infarction.
entries.displayTitle: Semaglutide reduces atrial fibrillation substrate in post-MI rats with heart failure
entries.journal: Europace : European pacing, arrhythmias, and cardiac electrophysiology : journal of the working groups on cardiac pacing, arrhythmias, and cardiac cellular electrophysiology of the European Society of Cardiology
entries.summary: In rats with reduced ejection fraction after myocardial infarction, semaglutide reduced atrial fibrillation inducibility, atrial fibrosis, inflammasome expression, and connexin-43 lateralization. Colchicine showed anti-inflammatory and anti-arrhythmic effects but did not prevent fibrosis or connexin remodeling.
keyFindings: Both semaglutide and colchicine reduced AF inducibility and duration in post-MI rats with ejection fraction ≤40%
Semaglutide uniquely attenuated atrial fibrosis and prevented connexin-43 lateralization
Semaglutide suppressed NLRP3 inflammasome and reduced SK4 potassium channel expression
Colchicine inhibited p38, JNK, and AKT signaling but did not prevent fibrosis or Cx43 remodeling
entries.limitations: Animal model with 21-day treatment duration may not fully reflect chronic atrial remodeling in human heart failure with atrial fibrillation.
entries.context: This extends emerging evidence for cardiovascular benefits of GLP-1 receptor agonists beyond metabolic effects. The findings suggest semaglutide may address atrial fibrillation substrate through anti-fibrotic and electrical remodeling mechanisms, which could complement its documented effects on ventricular function and clinical cardiovascular outcomes.

entries.title: Pilot study of epigenetic aging and treatment response to semaglutide in the SLIM LIVER study.
entries.displayTitle: Semaglutide responders show slowed epigenetic aging in HIV-MASLD trial
entries.studyType: Post hoc analysis of single-arm trial
entries.summary: Post hoc analysis of 41 people with HIV and MASLD receiving semaglutide 1.0 mg weekly for 24 weeks found that participants whose epigenetic pace of aging slowed (41.5%) had significantly greater liver fat reduction and trends toward improved gait speed.
keyFindings: Participants with decreased DunedinPACE (41.5%) showed greater liver fat reduction (p = 0.024) and trend toward improved gait speed.
Increased PCDNAmTL was associated with better gait speed improvement (p = 0.012).
Overall median DunedinPACE change was +0.018, PCGrimAge +0.54 years over 24 weeks.
Epigenetic aging biomarkers may predict semaglutide treatment responsiveness.
entries.limitations: Single-arm design with no placebo control, small sample (n=41), post hoc analysis, and relatively short 24-week duration limit causal inference about semaglutide's direct effects on epigenetic aging.
entries.context: This extends prior findings that semaglutide improves metabolic health and liver fat in MASLD by suggesting epigenetic aging markers may identify responders. The association between slowed biological aging and clinical outcomes is consistent with broader literature linking GLP-1 agonists to multi-system benefits.

entries.title: GLP-1 Receptor Agonist Use and Wound Outcomes After Free Flap Breast Reconstruction.
entries.displayTitle: GLP-1 agonists linked to fewer wound complications after breast reconstruction
entries.summary: Retrospective cohort study of 2012–2025 health records found patients on GLP-1 agonists within one year before free flap breast reconstruction had significantly lower wound complication rates (9.0% vs. 17.1%) compared to non-users, with reduced infections and dehiscence.
keyFindings: GLP-1 users had 47% lower composite wound complication rates (9.0% vs. 17.1%, p=0.002)
Surgical site infections reduced by half (4.1% vs. 8.1%, p=0.026)
Wound dehiscence occurred less frequently (3.8% vs. 7.8%, p=0.023)
Benefit most pronounced in non-diabetic patients (7.9% vs. 18.6% complications, p=0.007)
entries.limitations: Retrospective database study using administrative codes; GLP-1 exposure defined broadly as any prescription within one year preoperatively; no data on dosing, adherence, or perioperative continuation.
entries.context: This finding appears consistent with emerging evidence that GLP-1 agonists may have anti-inflammatory or wound-healing effects, though prior literature has been mixed. The study extends safety data to free flap breast reconstruction specifically.

entries.title: The effects of GLP-1 receptor agonists on Alzheimer's pathophysiology: A systematic review.
entries.displayTitle: GLP-1 agonists reduce Alzheimer's biomarkers in preclinical models
entries.summary: Systematic review of 30 preclinical and 2 clinical studies found GLP-1 receptor agonists consistently reduced amyloid-beta plaques and tau hyperphosphorylation in animal models. However, limited clinical trials showed no cognitive benefits despite some biomarker changes.
keyFindings: Liraglutide consistently reduced both amyloid-beta and tau pathology across animal and cell models
Semaglutide showed largely positive effects with four studies reporting reduced pathology, one showed no benefit
Two clinical trials failed to demonstrate cognitive improvement despite some biomarker changes
Dulaglutide consistently reduced tau phosphorylation and amyloid accumulation in mouse models
entries.limitations: Clinical evidence is very limited with only two trials reviewed, and neither demonstrated cognitive benefit despite preclinical data showing clear neuroprotective effects.
entries.context: GLP-1 receptor agonists are increasingly investigated for neurodegenerative diseases beyond their established role in diabetes. This review consolidates evidence of their effects on core Alzheimer's pathology, though it reveals a translational gap between preclinical promise and clinical outcomes.

entries.title: Dynamics of Body Composition and Metabolic Risk in Adolescents With Obesity Under GLP-1 Receptor Agonist Therapy.
entries.displayTitle: GLP-1 RAs improve body composition and metabolic syndrome markers in adolescents with obesity
entries.summary: Retrospective study of 67 adolescents (12–18 years) with obesity found those receiving GLP-1 RA therapy (liraglutide or semaglutide) plus lifestyle intervention showed greater improvements in BMI z-scores and muscle-to-fat ratios compared to lifestyle intervention alone, with changes linked to metabolic syndrome component improvement.
keyFindings: GLP-1 RA group achieved greater BMI z-score reduction (−0.34 vs. −0.07, p<0.001) and muscle-to-fat ratio improvement (0.41 vs. 0.13, p=0.007).
Treatment duration was the sole predictor of BMI and muscle-to-fat ratio improvements in multivariable analysis.
A 0.25-unit decrease in BMI z-score associated with 2.8-fold higher odds of metabolic syndrome component improvement.
entries.limitations: Retrospective design with non-randomized treatment assignment; individualized dosing precludes dose-response analysis; relatively small sample (n=67) from single center limits generalizability.
entries.context: Findings extend evidence that GLP-1 RAs improve weight and metabolic markers in pediatric obesity. The preservation of muscle-to-fat ratio during weight loss is consistent with adult data suggesting favorable body composition changes compared to diet alone.

entries.title: Associations of Semaglutide With Skeletal Outcomes in People With Obesity, With and Without Type 2 Diabetes: A Target Trial Emulation.
entries.displayTitle: Semaglutide linked to 16–31% lower fracture risk in obese patients with diabetes
entries.summary: This retrospective cohort study using electronic health records examined skeletal outcomes in people with obesity initiating semaglutide versus comparators. In patients with type 2 diabetes, semaglutide was associated with 16–31% lower major osteoporotic fracture risk over 3 years; no association was seen in non-diabetic patients.
keyFindings: In obese patients with T2D, semaglutide reduced major osteoporotic fracture risk by 16% vs. usual care (HR 0.84) and 28–31% vs. other glucose-lowering drugs.
No significant fracture risk reduction was observed in obese patients without T2D across any comparison.
Osteoporosis diagnosis rates did not differ significantly in most comparisons in either cohort.
Exploratory outcomes for osteoarthritis and gout showed inconsistent patterns across comparators.
entries.limitations: Retrospective design using electronic health records; residual confounding possible despite propensity matching; shorter follow-up (2 years) in the non-diabetic cohort; no bone density or mechanistic data.
entries.context: This extends prior GLP-1 receptor agonist research by suggesting skeletal benefits may be specific to patients with type 2 diabetes. The finding appears consistent with GLP-1's known effects on bone turnover markers, though the mechanism for diabetes-specific benefit remains unclear.

entries.title: Carbonless amino acids and a carbonless GHK peptide.
entries.displayTitle: Computational study replaces carbon atoms in GHK with boron-nitrogen pairs
entries.summary: Computational chemistry study substituted carbon with boron-nitrogen pairs in glycine, histidine, lysine, and GHK peptide to create "carbonless" analogues. Modeling predicts the carbonless GHK (cGHK) binds Cu(II) 6.24 kcal/mol more strongly than native GHK.
keyFindings: DFT calculations identified stable carbonless amino acid analogues (cGly, cHis, cLys) and carbonless GHK (cGHK)
cGHK showed broader conformational landscape than GHK, suggesting enhanced conformational plasticity
cGHK predicted to stabilize Cu(II) 6.24 kcal/mol more than native GHK in aqueous conditions
BN substitution retained structural logic while altering metal-binding thermodynamics
entries.limitations: Purely computational study with no experimental synthesis or validation of the proposed carbonless peptide structures or their predicted properties.
entries.context: GHK-Cu is known as a copper-binding peptide with regenerative properties. This theoretical study explores whether replacing carbon with isoelectronic boron-nitrogen pairs could create a carbon-free peptide analogue with altered metal-binding properties.

entries.title: Glycyl-L-histidyl-L-lysine-Cu2(GHK-Cu) Attenuates CuSOor LPS induced-inflammation in Zebrafish larvae model.
entries.displayTitle: GHK-Cu suppresses inflammation and oxidative stress in zebrafish larvae model
entries.summary: GHK-Cu demonstrated anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects in zebrafish larvae exposed to copper sulfate or LPS. The peptide reduced immune cell migration, pro-inflammatory cytokines, and oxidative stress markers while increasing anti-inflammatory IL-10 and SOD activity.
keyFindings: GHK-Cu decreased neutrophil and macrophage migration in CuSO₄- and LPS-induced inflammation models
Suppressed pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6) and increased anti-inflammatory IL-10 expression
Reduced nitric oxide and reactive oxygen species levels while improving SOD activity
Downregulated the JAK1 signaling pathway
entries.limitations: Zebrafish larvae model may not fully translate to human inflammatory conditions; no dose-response analysis or long-term effects reported in abstract.
entries.context: GHK-Cu is known primarily for dermatological applications, but this study extends its profile by demonstrating anti-inflammatory and antioxidant mechanisms in an acute inflammation model. The findings are consistent with prior observations of tissue repair properties.

entries.title: Safety and Efficacy of Approved and Unapproved Peptide Therapies for Musculoskeletal Injuries and Athletic Performance.
entries.displayTitle: Narrative review examines safety gaps in unapproved sports-medicine peptides
entries.summary: This narrative review evaluates the pharmacology, safety, and regulatory status of approved and unapproved peptides marketed for musculoskeletal injury recovery and athletic performance. While many show promise in animal models, human safety data remain scarce for most unapproved compounds.
keyFindings: Many unapproved peptides demonstrate favorable tissue repair and metabolic outcomes in animal models
Rigorous human safety data are scarce for most unapproved peptides, with potential for serious harm
Review covers framework for evidence-based discussions with patients about peptide use
Social media amplifies placebo effects in peptide efficacy perceptions
entries.limitations: As a narrative review, does not systematically evaluate or grade evidence quality across studies.
entries.context: This review synthesizes current knowledge on peptides increasingly used in sports medicine, addressing the gap between FDA-approved compounds with established safety profiles and a growing gray market of unregulated peptides marketed directly to consumers seeking accelerated recovery or performance enhancement.

entries.title: Are serum MOTS-c levels and MOTS-c m.1382A>C polymorphism related to polycystic ovary syndrome?
entries.displayTitle: MOTS-c levels similar in PCOS and healthy adolescents
entries.summary: Case-control study of 121 adolescents with PCOS and 125 controls found no significant difference in serum MOTS-c levels (p=0.059) or association with metabolic parameters. All participants had wild-type m.1382A>C genotype.
keyFindings: Mean MOTS-c levels trended higher in PCOS but did not reach statistical significance (p=0.059)
No association between MOTS-c levels and anthropometric or metabolic parameters in PCOS group
All 246 participants had wild-type A/A genotype; no m.1382A>C polymorphism detected
Findings suggest MOTS-c may have minor involvement in PCOS pathophysiology
entries.limitations: Absence of the m.1382A>C variant in all participants limits ability to assess polymorphism-disease associations; results may not generalize to populations with different genetic backgrounds.
entries.context: MOTS-c is a mitochondrial peptide linked to insulin sensitivity and metabolic regulation. This is the first study examining MOTS-c levels and the m.1382A>C polymorphism in adolescent PCOS, extending prior diabetes-focused research to reproductive endocrinology.

entries.title: Therapeutic peptides in gerontology: mechanisms and applications for healthy aging.
entries.displayTitle: Narrative review maps mechanisms and applications of peptides in aging
entries.summary: This narrative review evaluates nine therapeutic peptides targeting aging hallmarks including metabolic dysfunction, tissue repair, and hormonal decline. FDA-approved peptides showed robust safety profiles, while investigational peptides like BPC-157, TB-500, GHK-Cu, CJC-1295, and ipamorelin demonstrated promising preclinical evidence but lack long-term validation.
keyFindings: Nine peptides identified spanning metabolic restoration, telomere biology, dermal regeneration, tissue repair, neuroprotection, and growth hormone modulation
FDA-approved agents demonstrated robust safety from large-scale trials; non-approved peptides lack long-term safety data
Significant knowledge gaps include optimal dosing, combination therapy effects, and efficacy biomarkers
Investigational peptides require rigorous clinical trials to establish safety and efficacy for healthspan extension
entries.limitations: Narrative review methodology lacks systematic quality appraisal; only 20 primary sources selected; cannot quantitatively synthesize evidence across heterogeneous peptides and aging endpoints.
entries.context: This review synthesizes current knowledge on therapeutic peptides in gerontology, identifying both clinically validated approaches and investigational candidates. It highlights the gap between promising preclinical evidence for peptides like BPC-157 and TB-500 and the need for systematic human validation.

entries.title: Combined antiretroviral therapy with low- or normal-protein, high-calorie diets appears to induce significant deleterious electrocardiographic changes in a rodent model.
entries.displayTitle: Tesamorelin prevents cART-induced cardiac fibrosis in rats on high-calorie diets
entries.journal: Brazilian journal of medical and biological research = Revista brasileira de pesquisas medicas e biologicas
entries.summary: In Sprague Dawley rats fed calorie-dense diets, antiretroviral therapy (dolutegravir-based or classical cART) caused ECG abnormalities and myocardial fibrosis. Co-administration of tesamorelin prevented these cardiac effects, suggesting growth hormone pathway involvement.
keyFindings: DTG-based and classical cART regimens combined with calorie-dense diets caused significant ECG changes and myocardial fibrosis (P<0.0001)
Tesamorelin co-treatment prevented cART-induced cardiac fibrosis and ECG abnormalities
Effects were observed with both low-protein and normal-protein calorie-dense diets
Findings implicate growth hormone pathway dysfunction in cART-related cardiac pathology
entries.limitations: Animal model with artificial dietary conditions; translatability to human HIV patients on standard diets and long-term cART requires clinical validation.
entries.context: Tesamorelin, a growth hormone-releasing hormone analog, is approved for HIV-associated lipodystrophy. This study extends its potential cardiac benefits in the context of antiretroviral therapy, consistent with growth hormone's known cardioprotective effects.
